perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign nondisclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information with reasonable care for the benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall restrict access to Confidential Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'strictest confidence', 'sole and exclusive', 'carefully restrict', and removing the requirement for third parties to sign nondisclosure agreements, the modified text weakens the protection of confidential information. This creates potential legal issues especially in states with stringent trade secret laws like California where 'reasonable steps' are required to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language weakens this requirement by changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable care' and removing the requirement for third parties to sign nondisclosure agreements, making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove they took sufficient steps to protect their confidential information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting California law regarding trade secrets. The original text requires 'strictest confidence' and 'carefully restrict' access, whereas the modified text uses 'reasonable care' and 'restrict access... as is deemed appropriate.' The original text also mandated nondisclosure agreements for third parties, a requirement that has been removed entirely. The Law Explanation argues that by changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable care' and removing the NDA requirement, it becomes more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove they took sufficient steps to protect their confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' and refers to 'a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the protective measures, the modified contract arguably makes it harder to prove that the Disclosing Party actively maintained secrecy. This creates a higher risk of potential misappropriation of trade secrets, because less security creates less protection.

perturbation.original_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the conditions under which the Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information ends (i.e., when it no longer qualifies as a trade secret or upon written release), the modified text creates an indefinite obligation. While seemingly strengthening the NDA, this could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under state laws that require reasonable time limitations for non-disclosure agreements, particularly when dealing with information that may eventually become publicly available or lose its trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (First) of Contracts Section 516 states that a restraint on trade is unreasonable if it is greater than is required for the protection of the person for whose benefit the restraint is imposed. By removing the conditions of when the confidentiality ends, the modified NDA could be deemed a greater restraint on trade than necessary, making it unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text contains conditions under which the Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information ends. The changed text removes these conditions, creating an indefinite obligation. The Law Explanation uses Restatement (First) of Contracts Section 516 to explain that a restraint on trade is unreasonable if it is greater than required for the protection of the person for whose benefit the restraint is imposed. The original text included an 'escape clause' for the receiving party, and removing it is a contradiction as it could create an unreasonable restraint on trade and be deemed unenforceable. The missing scraped snippets doesn't change this determination.

perturbation.original_text: Employee is provided notice that an individual shall not be held criminally or civilly liable under any federal or state trade secret law for the disclosure of a trade secret that is made (i) in confidence to a federal, state, or local government official, either directly or indirectly, or to an attorney; and (ii) solely for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law; or is made in a complaint or other document filed in a lawsuit or other proceeding, if such filing is made under seal. An individual who files a lawsuit for retaliation by an employer for reporting a suspected violation of law may disclose the trade secret to the attorney of the individual and use the trade secret information in the court proceeding, if the individual (i) files any document containing the trade secret under seal; and (ii) does not disclose the trade secret, except pursuant t o court order.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee is provided notice that an individual may, under certain circumstances, report suspected violations of law.
perturbation.explanation: This section provides a safe harbor for whistleblowers under federal and state trade secret laws, referencing the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). By significantly shortening and generalizing this clause, the modified text removes specific protections afforded to whistleblowers who report trade secret violations. This omission could deter employees from reporting illegal activities, fearing reprisal, and removes the clear legal protection provided by the DTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides immunity for whistleblowers who disclose trade secrets to government officials or in court filings under seal for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law. The modified text, by removing this specific notice, may discourage employees from reporting potential violations, thus undermining the intent and protections offered by the DTSA.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1833 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1833 : Exceptions to prohibitions Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1833. Exceptions to prohibitions (a) In General .-This chapter does not prohibit or create a private right of action for- (1) any otherwise lawful activity conducted by a governmental entity of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State; or (2) the disclosure of a trade secret in accordance with subsection (b). (b) Immunity From Liability for Confidential Disclosure of a Trade Secret to the Gov
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a safe harbor for whistleblowers under trade secret laws, referencing the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). By shortening and generalizing this clause, the modified text removes the specific protections afforded to whistleblowers. The law snippet (18 USC 1833) outlines exceptions to prohibitions related to trade secrets, specifically mentioning the disclosure of a trade secret under certain conditions. The omission of these specific protections in the modified text directly contradicts the intent and provisions of the DTSA, as it removes a legally mandated protection for whistleblowers, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
